is an [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") [stadium](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), Pennsylvania, U.S. It is the home stadium of the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the [Temple Owls football](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football") team of [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_University "Temple University"). The stadium is located in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia") on Pattison Avenue between 11th and South Darien streets alongside [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 95 in Pennsylvania"). It is part of the [South Philadelphia Sports Complex](/wiki/South_Philadelphia_Sports_Complex "South Philadelphia Sports Complex") and has a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 67,594.

**Lincoln Financial Field** is an [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") [stadium](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), Pennsylvania, U.S. It is the home stadium of the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the [Temple Owls football](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football") team of [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_University "Temple University"). The stadium is located in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia") on Pattison Avenue between 11th and South Darien streets alongside [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 95 in Pennsylvania"). It is part of the [South Philadelphia Sports Complex](/wiki/South_Philadelphia_Sports_Complex "South Philadelphia Sports Complex") and has a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 67,594.

The stadium opened on August 3, 2003, after two years of construction that began on May 7, 2001, replacing [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium"), which opened in 1971 and served as the home field for both the Eagles and [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") through 2002 and 2003, respectively. While total [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") is similar to that of Veterans Stadium, the new stadium includes double the number of luxury and wheelchair-accessible seats and more modern services. The field's construction included several [LED](/wiki/LED "LED") video displays and more than 624 feet (190 m) of LED ribbon boards.[[6]](#cite_note-technology-6)

[Naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights") were sold in June 2002 to the [Lincoln Financial Group](/wiki/Lincoln_Financial_Group "Lincoln Financial Group"), for a sum of $139.6 million over 21 years. The City of Philadelphia and the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania cumulatively contributed approximately $188 million in public funding to the stadium construction.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Additional construction funding was raised from the sale of stadium builder's licenses, which are necessary to purchase season tickets for some of the stadium's best seating levels.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The City of Philadelphia owns the stadium due to the sizable public contribution, but the Eagles lease the stadium from the city as the sole operator of the stadium, giving them full control over the stadium's operations.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [Army–Navy](/wiki/Army%E2%80%93Navy_Game "Army–Navy Game") [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") game is frequently played at the stadium due to Philadelphia being located halfway between both service academies, the stadium being able to house the large crowds in attendance, and the historic nature of the city. [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_University "Temple University")'s [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") college football [team](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football") also plays their home games at Lincoln Financial Field, paying the Eagles $3 million a year to do so as of February 2020.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [Philadelphia Union](/wiki/Philadelphia_Union "Philadelphia Union") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") have played exhibition games here against high-profile international clubs when their stadium [Subaru Park](/wiki/Subaru_Park "Subaru Park") does not provide adequate seating. The stadium also plays host to several soccer games each year and will host matches during the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup"). It has also played host to the [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") lacrosse national championship five times: 2005, 2006, 2013, 2019, and 2023.

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Features")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=). *(August 2023)* |

The design of the stadium is meant to evoke the team's namesake eagle with wing like canopies above the east and west stands and the Eagle's Nest balcony beyond the north end-zone. The team's primary eagle logo is patterned in the dark green seats of the upper deck of the east and west stands. Three open corners of the stadium provide fans with views of the Philadelphia skyline and the field. The exterior of the stadium uses a brick façade to reference the historic brick architecture present throughout Philadelphia, while the exposed steel structure evokes the city's bridges and future.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In late spring 2013, the Eagles announced that there would be some major upgrades to Lincoln Financial Field over the next two years. The total project estimate was valued at over $125 million. The upgrades included seating expansion, two new HD video boards, upgraded amenities, WiFi, and two new connecting bridges for upper levels. These upgrades were decided upon after research from season ticket holders, advisory boards, and fan focus groups. The majority of these changes, including WiFi (which would accommodate 45,000 users and have coverage over the entire stadium), were completed by the 2013 home opener. The upgraded sound systems and video boards were finished for the 2014 season.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Suites and lounges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Suites and lounges")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/TheLinc-outside.jpg/250px-TheLinc-outside.jpg)](/wiki/File:TheLinc-outside.jpg)

Lincoln Financial Field seen from [I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 95 in Pennsylvania")

There are 172 luxury suites at Lincoln Financial Field. They range in capacity from 12 to 40 people and cost $75,000 to $300,000 per year or $20,000 for a single game rental. The suites are located in six separate areas throughout the stadium. There are 3,040 luxury suite seats in total.[[13]](#cite_note-facts-13)

There are two exclusive 40,000 sq ft (3,700 m2) club lounges in the stadium. The lounge on the west side is the "Hyundai Club" and the one on the east side is the "Tork Club". Suite holders and club seat holders have access to these 2 lounges. There are a total of 10,828 club seats in the stadium. These club seats feature some unique benefits above and beyond the lounges. One major benefit is the lounges open hours before the event, in most cases 2–4 hours. The lounges also have multiple full-service bars in them. The seats in the club level are padded, and there is also a wait staff present to serve food and drinks.[[13]](#cite_note-facts-13)

## Parking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Parking")]

There are numerous parking lots surrounding Lincoln Financial Field. The parking assignments may change depending on other activities in the area on a particular day. According to the stadium's website, all lots cost visitors $45 and $90 for an oversized vehicle. Tailgating is allowed in all lots except for T-X lots. The majority of the lots are public cash lots.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Lincoln_Financial_Field%2C_Philadelphia.jpg/250px-Lincoln_Financial_Field%2C_Philadelphia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_Financial_Field,_Philadelphia.jpg)

The [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") visiting Lincoln Financial Field to play the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") in Week 10 of the [2015 NFL season](/wiki/2015_NFL_season "2015 NFL season")

* **August 3, 2003**: Lincoln Financial Field hosted its first ticketed event, a soccer match between [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") and [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona")
* **August 22, 2003**: The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") hosted the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") in the first pre-season football game at Lincoln Financial Field, with the Patriots defeating the Eagles 24–12.
* **September 6, 2003**: Lincoln Financial Field hosted its first regular-season college football game, a college matchup of local Philadelphia rivals, [Villanova](/wiki/Villanova_Wildcats_football "Villanova Wildcats football") and [Temple](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football"). Villanova prevailed 23–20 in double overtime.
* **September 8, 2003**: The Eagles and the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") competed on *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* in the first regular-season NFL game at Lincoln Financial Field. The game was referenced as the "Inaugural Game" at Lincoln Financial Field. The [Eagles](/wiki/2003_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2003 Philadelphia Eagles season") were shut out by a score of 17–0 in their new home by the [Buccaneers](/wiki/2003_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2003 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season"). Tampa Bay wide receiver [Joe Jurevicius](/wiki/Joe_Jurevicius "Joe Jurevicius") scored the stadium's first touchdown in the third quarter.
* **September 14, 2003**: [Duce Staley](/wiki/Duce_Staley "Duce Staley") scored the first Eagles points at the new stadium, a two-yard touchdown run in the second quarter of a 31–10 loss to the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2003_New_England_Patriots_season "2003 New England Patriots season").
* **January 11, 2004**: NFL Divisional Playoff game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") 20, [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") 17). This game is also known as "The Miracle of [4th and 26](/wiki/4th_and_26 "4th and 26")". [Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb") connected on a 28-yard pass to receiver [Freddie Mitchell](/wiki/Freddie_Mitchell "Freddie Mitchell") on 4th and 26 late in the 4th quarter with the Eagles out of timeouts. This led to the game-tying field goal that sent the game into overtime. In the overtime period, [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") tossed an interception to [Brian Dawkins](/wiki/Brian_Dawkins "Brian Dawkins"), which set up [David Akers](/wiki/David_Akers "David Akers")' game-deciding 37-yard [field goal](/wiki/Field_goal "Field goal") that sent the Eagles to their third straight NFC Championship Game.
* **May 2004**: Minor modifications were made to the stadium to change capacity slightly. Some seats were removed from the club box level to increase handicap access. The temporary seats were removed in the north east end zone and a permanent structure was erected and formally named The Pepsi Zone. It is designed as a family-friendly section.
* **January 23, 2005**: NFC Championship Game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") 27, [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") 10). Eagles advance to the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX") for the first time since [1980](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XV "Super Bowl XV").
* **April 10, 2010**: The [Philadelphia Union](/wiki/Philadelphia_Union "Philadelphia Union") win their inaugural home opener, a 3–2 victory over [D.C. United](/wiki/D.C._United "D.C. United"). A second match was played against [FC Dallas](/wiki/FC_Dallas "FC Dallas") on May 15, ending in a 1–1 tie; those games served as home games before the opening of [Subaru Park](/wiki/Subaru_Park "Subaru Park") June 27 against [Seattle Sounders FC](/wiki/Seattle_Sounders_FC "Seattle Sounders FC").
* **July 21, 2010**: [Philadelphia Union](/wiki/Philadelphia_Union "Philadelphia Union") hosted [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") on their North America tour. Manchester United won, 1–0.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* **July 26, 2015**: In the [2015 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2015_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2015 CONCACAF Gold Cup") final, [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team") defeated [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_national_football_team "Jamaica national football team") 3–1 in a nearly sold-out game with 68,930 in attendance.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* **October 31, 2015**: Temple hosts [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Irish_football "Notre Dame Irish football") on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") Saturday Night Football on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") in primetime in front of a sold-out record crowd of 69,280. Notre Dame defeated Temple 24–20.
* **January 21, 2018**: NFC Championship Game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") 38, [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") 7). Eagles advance to the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") for the first time in [thirteen years](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX").
* **September 6, 2018**: [NFL Kickoff](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff "NFL Kickoff") Game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") 18, [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") 12). The Super Bowl champion Philadelphia Eagles defeated the Atlanta Falcons in a rematch of their 2017 NFC Divisional Playoff matchup.
* **February 23, 2019**: In the [2019 NHL Stadium Series](/wiki/2019_NHL_Stadium_Series "2019 NHL Stadium Series"), the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") defeated the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") 4–3 in overtime, in front of a crowd of 69,620, the largest recorded attendance for a hockey game in Pennsylvania.
* **November 20, 2019**: The Linc hosted a [high school football](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football") game between the [Pleasantville Greyhounds](/wiki/Pleasantville_High_School_(New_Jersey) "Pleasantville High School (New Jersey)") and [Camden Panthers](/wiki/Camden_High_School_(New_Jersey) "Camden High School (New Jersey)") in a resumption of a game that was interrupted on November 15 due to a shooting that left one dead.
* **January 29, 2023**: NFC Championship Game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") 31, [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") 7). The Eagles won in dominant fashion against the 49ers to advance to [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII").
* **April 6–7, 2024**: [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE") held their 40th annual [WrestleMania](/wiki/WrestleMania "WrestleMania"), the largest annual [pro-wrestling](/wiki/Pro-wrestling "Pro-wrestling") event, at this venue. The event was held across 2 nights. [WrestleMania XL](/wiki/WrestleMania_XL "WrestleMania XL") holds significance as the first pro-wrestling event taking place in the stadium.
* **January 26, 2025:** NFC Championship Game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2024_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2024 Philadelphia Eagles season") 55, [Washington Commanders](/wiki/2024_Washington_Commanders_season "2024 Washington Commanders season") 23). The Eagles dominated the Commanders to win their 5th NFC title and 6th overall conference title in franchise history to advance to [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX").
* **September 4, 2025**: [NFL Kickoff](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff "NFL Kickoff") Game ([Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") 24, [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") 20). The Super Bowl champion Philadelphia Eagles defeated their longtime rival Dallas Cowboys.

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Controversies")]

* For the inaugural season at Lincoln Financial Field (2003), the Eagles imposed a ban on hoagies and [cheesesteaks](/wiki/Cheesesteak "Cheesesteak") being brought into the stadium, citing security concerns related to the [events of September 11](/wiki/September_11,_2001_attacks "September 11, 2001 attacks"). The ban only lasted one week after much mockery by fans and [sports radio](/wiki/Sports_radio "Sports radio"), along with traditional media.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* Prior to the first Eagles game of the 2007 season, a ruling was made concerning a Philadelphia and national tradition: [tailgating](/wiki/Tailgate_party "Tailgate party"). While the Eagles did not ban the act entirely, they did ban the use of tables and tents as well as the purchasing of more than one parking spot per vehicle. Prices were also doubled to $40 for [RVs](/wiki/RVs "RVs") and [buses](/wiki/Bus "Bus"), and $20 for cars. Fans have been reported to be upset.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Training camp

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Training camp")]

The Eagles decided after the 2012 season to move training camp from [Lehigh University](/wiki/Lehigh_University "Lehigh University") in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem,_Pennsylvania "Bethlehem, Pennsylvania") to Philadelphia. As a part of this new agreement, the Eagles will have multiple practices, which are open to the public, at Lincoln Financial Field. The rest of the practices will be closed, and will take place across the street at the NovaCare Complex.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## College football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: College football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Defense.gov_photo_essay_101211-A-3001M-0041.jpg/250px-Defense.gov_photo_essay_101211-A-3001M-0041.jpg)](/wiki/File:Defense.gov_photo_essay_101211-A-3001M-0041.jpg)

The [Army–Navy Game](/wiki/Army%E2%80%93Navy_Game "Army–Navy Game") at Lincoln Financial Field in 2010

Lincoln Financial Field is the home field for [Temple University football](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football"). On August 13, 2003, the Philadelphia Eagles and Temple University announced a 15-year agreement for Temple to play their home football games at Lincoln Financial Field.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Temple played its first game at the Linc on September 6, 2003, against [Villanova](/wiki/Villanova_Wildcats_football "Villanova Wildcats football"), the teams' first meeting since 1980. During the 2015 season, the Temple Owls sold out the Linc on two occasions. The September 5 season opener against [Penn State](/wiki/2015_Penn_State_Nittany_Lions_football_team "2015 Penn State Nittany Lions football team"), and again on October 31 in a prime time matchup against [Notre Dame.](/wiki/2015_Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football_team "2015 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team") With its capacity of 67,594, it is the largest stadium in the [American Athletic Conference](/wiki/American_Athletic_Conference "American Athletic Conference").

Lincoln Financial Field is the primary home to the [Army–Navy Game](/wiki/Army%E2%80%93Navy_Game "Army–Navy Game"). The game has been played the most often in Philadelphia. It was played at [Veterans Stadium](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium") for the final time in 2001, and prior to the Vet, at [John F. Kennedy Stadium](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Stadium "John F. Kennedy Stadium") and [Franklin Field](/wiki/Franklin_Field "Franklin Field"). The Linc hosted the game five times between 2003 and 2009. It was announced on June 9, 2009, that the game would be played in Philadelphia at the Linc in 2010, 2012, 2013, 2015 and 2017.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On October 30, 2025, Lincoln Financial Field hosted an [HBCU](/wiki/HBCU "HBCU") rivalry game between [Delaware State University](/wiki/Delaware_State_University "Delaware State University") and [Norfolk State University](/wiki/Norfolk_State_University "Norfolk State University"). The college teams' head coaches were both former Eagles players: for DSU, [DeSean Jackson](/wiki/DeSean_Jackson "DeSean Jackson") and for NSU, [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick"). The game had been publicized as "Battle of the Legends" and occurred on a rainy and windy day as remnants from [Hurricane Melissa](/wiki/Hurricane_Melissa "Hurricane Melissa") moved past the U.S. Atlantic coast.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Delaware State University](/wiki/Delaware_State_University "Delaware State University") won 27–20.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Lincoln Financial Field is one of only five stadiums to be the home of both an NFL and college team; the other four are [Allegiant Stadium](/wiki/Allegiant_Stadium "Allegiant Stadium") in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") ([Raiders](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Raiders "Las Vegas Raiders") and [UNLV](/wiki/UNLV_Rebels_football "UNLV Rebels football")), [Hard Rock Stadium](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Stadium "Hard Rock Stadium") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami") ([Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") and [Hurricanes](/wiki/Miami_Hurricanes_football "Miami Hurricanes football")), [Acrisure Stadium](/wiki/Acrisure_Stadium "Acrisure Stadium") in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") ([Steelers](/wiki/Steelers "Steelers") and [Panthers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Panthers_football "Pittsburgh Panthers football")), and [Raymond James Stadium](/wiki/Raymond_James_Stadium "Raymond James Stadium") in [Tampa](/wiki/Tampa "Tampa") ([Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") and [South Florida](/wiki/South_Florida_Bulls_football "South Florida Bulls football")).

## Soccer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Soccer")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/USA-Turkey-Linc.jpg/960px-USA-Turkey-Linc.jpg)](/wiki/File:USA-Turkey-Linc.jpg "Panorama of the U.S. National Soccer Team playing the national team of Turkey in the 2010 World Cup send-off series")

Panorama of the U.S. National Soccer Team playing the national team of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") in the [2010 World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") send-off series

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/VEN-URU_9-6-2016.jpg/250px-VEN-URU_9-6-2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:VEN-URU_9-6-2016.jpg)

[Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team") vs. [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela_national_football_team "Venezuela national football team") soccer match at the stadium in June 2016

The stadium opened on August 3, 2003, with 68,396 attending a preseason friendly with [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") defeating [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") 3–1.[[25]](#cite_note-25) It hosted some [2003 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2003 FIFA Women's World Cup") matches.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2004, after winning the gold-medal in [soccer](/wiki/Football_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Football at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Women's tournament") at the [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics"), the [U.S. women's team](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_soccer_team "United States women's national soccer team") conducted a "Fan Celebration Tour", playing ten matches across the United States from September to December 2004. The matches were the final national team appearances for [Mia Hamm](/wiki/Mia_Hamm "Mia Hamm"), [Joy Fawcett](/wiki/Joy_Fawcett "Joy Fawcett"), and [Julie Foudy](/wiki/Julie_Foudy "Julie Foudy"), all of whom retired after the tour. The team played at the Linc on November 6, 2004, where they lost 3–1 to Denmark in front of 14,812 spectators.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In late July and early August 2004, Manchester United returned to play [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_FC "Celtic FC") and [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan") played [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") in two friendly matches.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

On July 18, 2009, Lincoln Financial Field hosted a doubleheader quarterfinal for the [2009 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2009_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2009 CONCACAF Gold Cup"). The first game was between [Canada](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Canada men's national soccer team") and [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras_national_football_team "Honduras national football team"), and the second between the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") and [Panama](/wiki/Panama_national_football_team "Panama national football team"). This was the first full international appearance for the United States national soccer team in Philadelphia since a 1968 friendly against [Israel](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team") at [Temple Stadium](/wiki/Temple_Stadium "Temple Stadium").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Lincoln Financial Field was listed by U.S. Soccer's World Cup bid committee as one of 18 stadiums to be potential sites for the United States to host the 2022 FIFA World Cup.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The Linc was also the first home of the [Philadelphia Union](/wiki/Philadelphia_Union "Philadelphia Union") early in 2010. The Union played their home opener against [D.C. United](/wiki/D.C._United "D.C. United") at the Linc on April 10, 2010, due to construction delays at their new stadium, PPL Park (now [Subaru Park](/wiki/Subaru_Park "Subaru Park")). The Union defeated United, 3–2. The Union also played [FC Dallas](/wiki/FC_Dallas "FC Dallas") to a 1–1 tie in the second home game in franchise history at the Linc. After moving to their permanent venue in Chester, the Philadelphia Union continued to use the Linc for matches where ticket demand is expected to far exceed the full capacity at its regular home until 2011.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] English powerhouse club Manchester United met the Philadelphia Union during their pre-season tour on July 21, 2010, with Manchester United defeating the Union by a score of 1–0.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Union played the Spanish powerhouse club [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") on July 24, 2011, with Real Madrid prevailing, 2–1.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Lincoln Financial Field also hosted the US National team's final match on home soil before the 2010 World Cup.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The US defeated [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") 2–1 on May 29, 2010.

The US men's national team returned to Lincoln Financial Field on August 10, 2011, for an international friendly match against Mexico, where they tied 1-1.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In 2012, the stadium hosted a World Football Challenge match between [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") and [Celtic F.C.](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") with Real Madrid winning 2–0.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

On August 2, 2014, Lincoln Financial Field hosted a soccer match between [A.S. Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") and [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") which was part of the [2014 International Champions Cup](/wiki/2014_International_Champions_Cup "2014 International Champions Cup"). Inter Milan won the match 2–0.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

On July 26, 2015, the Linc hosted the final match of the [2015 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2015_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2015 CONCACAF Gold Cup").

In 2016, the stadium hosted games in the [Copa América Centenario](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica_Centenario "Copa América Centenario").

The Linc hosted a doubleheader of quarterfinals of the [2017 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2017_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2017 CONCACAF Gold Cup") and did so again for the [2019 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2019_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2019 CONCACAF Gold Cup").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

On July 25, 2018, the Linc hosted a [2018 International Champions Cup](/wiki/2018_International_Champions_Cup "2018 International Champions Cup") match between [Juventus FC](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") and [FC Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich") that Juventus won 2–0.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

On August 29, 2019, the Linc hosted the [US Women's National Team](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_soccer_team "United States women's national soccer team") in the second match of their victory tour in a game against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_women%27s_national_football_team "Portugal women's national football team"). The match set the record for the highest attendance for the team in a friendly match, attracting 49,504 fans. The United States won 4–0.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

On July 31, 2024, Lincoln Financial Field hosted a friendly between [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") and [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), with Liverpool winning 2–1. The match attracted a total of 69,879 fans, setting the record for the highest-attended soccer match in the stadium's history.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### 2026 FIFA World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2026 FIFA World Cup")]

Lincoln Financial Field will host matches during the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup"), making it one of the eleven selected U.S. venues. ## Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Other sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/2019_NHL_Stadium_Series_at_Lincoln_Financial_Field_in_Philadelphia.jpg/250px-2019_NHL_Stadium_Series_at_Lincoln_Financial_Field_in_Philadelphia.jpg)](/wiki/File:2019_NHL_Stadium_Series_at_Lincoln_Financial_Field_in_Philadelphia.jpg)

The [2019 NHL Stadium Series](/wiki/2019_NHL_Stadium_Series "2019 NHL Stadium Series") game after the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Philadelphia_Flyers_season "2018–19 Philadelphia Flyers season") defeated the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Pittsburgh_Penguins_season "2018–19 Pittsburgh Penguins season") 4–3 in [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(ice_hockey) "Overtime (ice hockey)")

The [NCAA Division I Men's Lacrosse Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Lacrosse_Championship "NCAA Division I Men's Lacrosse Championship") in 2005, 2006, 2013, 2015, 2016, 2019, 2023, and 2024 were held at the stadium.[[44]](#cite_note-44) In 2020, the event was scheduled to take place in May but was canceled due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

The [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") celebrated their [2008 World Series](/wiki/2008_World_Series "2008 World Series") championship with a parade down [Broad Street](/wiki/Broad_Street_(Philadelphia) "Broad Street (Philadelphia)"), appearance before a sold-out crowd at the Linc, and then a ceremony at [Citizens Bank Park](/wiki/Citizens_Bank_Park "Citizens Bank Park"). The Citizens Bank Park ceremony was simulcast to the crowd at the Linc.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Tickets to the event at Lincoln Financial Field were made available at no cost to the public and were gone within 45 minutes when they were made available at 3pm on October 30, 2008.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[Monster Jam](/wiki/Monster_Jam "Monster Jam") performed at the stadium from 2010 to 2012 as part of the summer stadium tour. After a six-year absence, Monster Jam returned to the stadium on May 5, 2018. Previous events took place during the winter at the [Spectrum](/wiki/Spectrum_(Philadelphia) "Spectrum (Philadelphia)") and the [Wells Fargo Center](/wiki/Wells_Fargo_Center_(Philadelphia) "Wells Fargo Center (Philadelphia)") from 2013 to 2015. The Linc would host [Monster Jam](/wiki/Monster_Jam "Monster Jam") again in 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025.

On February 23, 2019, the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") defeated the [Pittsburgh Penguins](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Penguins "Pittsburgh Penguins") 4–3 in overtime in the [2019 NHL Stadium Series](/wiki/2019_NHL_Stadium_Series "2019 NHL Stadium Series") in front of a crowd of 69,620, the largest record attendance for a hockey game in Pennsylvania.

On July 27, 2022, it was announced that Lincoln Financial Field would host [WrestleMania XL](/wiki/WrestleMania_XL "WrestleMania XL"), on April 6, and April 7, 2024.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### In TV and Movies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: In TV and Movies")]

The stadium is prominently featured in the opening of the TV show *[It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia](/wiki/It%27s_Always_Sunny_in_Philadelphia "It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia")*. It also features in the second episode of season 3 of the show.
The stadium also appeared in the 2012 movie *[Silver Linings Playbook](/wiki/Silver_Linings_Playbook "Silver Linings Playbook")*.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

In *[The Walking Dead: The Ones Who Live](/wiki/The_Walking_Dead:_The_Ones_Who_Live "The Walking Dead: The Ones Who Live")*, the stadium was destroyed in an airstrike during the early days of the zombie apocalypse by U.S. Air Force pilot Donald Okafor, killing 4,000 U.S. Marines, including his own wife, as mentioned in episode 1 of the show. In the same episode, Okafor uses the ruins of the stadium to hold a secret meeting with [Rick Grimes](/wiki/Rick_Grimes "Rick Grimes") and Pearl Thorne. Rick later returns to collect a zombie corpse for his escape plan. In episode 3, Thorne uses the ruins to meet with Rick and [Michonne](/wiki/Michonne "Michonne") in secret.

It was featured on the finale of [The Amazing Race 36](/wiki/The_Amazing_Race_36 "The Amazing Race 36").

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincoln_Financial_Field&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Awards")]

In 2013, Lincoln Financial Field was considered one of the "greenest" NFL stadiums.[[67]](#cite_note-Energy_Gov-67) Energy-efficient additions include 11,000 solar panels, and 14 [UGE](/wiki/UGE_International "UGE International")-4K wind turbines outside and on top of the stadium respectively. These installations account for 30% of the electricity used to run the facility. 2013 also marked the fourth year in a row that the venue made the top of [PETA](/wiki/PETA "PETA")'s list of "Vegetarian-Friendly NFL Stadiums".[[68]](#cite_note-68)
